claims.claim: Decomposition of LiPF6-containing electrolytes under thermal heating conditions causes emissions of toxic gases hydrogen fluoride (HF) and phosphoryl fluoride (POF3), where POF3 can be considered a precursor of HF in humid environments.
claims.quote: Decomposition of LiPF6-containing electrolytes under thermal heating conditions has been found to cause emissions of toxic gases such as hydrogen ﬂuoride, HF, and phosphoryl ﬂuoride, POF3, which, in humid environments, can be considered a precursor of HF

claims.claim: Non-cyclic carbonates (DMC, DEC, EA) evaporate at temperatures below 100°C, while cyclic carbonates (EC and PC) have negligible vapor pressure at low temperature and evaporate at temperatures above 100°C.
claims.quote: the non-cyclic carbonates are more volatile and will evaporate at temperatures below 100 (cid:3)C, while the cyclic carbonates (EC and PC) have negligible vapor pressure at low temperature and evaporate at temperatures above 100 (cid:3)C

claims.claim: Emissions of HF and POF3 were simultaneously detected from 100°C to 170°C during thermal heating of extracted Li-ion battery electrolyte containing LiPF6.
claims.quote: Regarding the decomposition of LiPF6, emissions of HF and one of its precursors, POF3, have been distinctly and almost simultaneously detected in the TG/FT-IR analysis from 100 (cid:3)C to 170 (cid:3)C

claims.claim: The simultaneous appearance and decrease of HF and POF3 emissions following a similar pattern may be explained by insufficient residual humidity to convert POF3 into HF.
claims.quote: The fact that the emissions of both HF and POF3 simultaneously appear around 100 (cid:3)C and decrease at 170 (cid:3)C following a similar pattern might be explained by the fact that the residual amount of humidity was insufﬁcient to convert POF3 in HF

claims.claim: No vibrational bands for PF5 were observed during analysis because PF5 is likely too short-lived due to its instability and tendency to react with residual water or organic solvents.
claims.quote: No vibrational bands for PF5 were observed in the analysis, as it probably is too short-lived because of its instability and tendency to react with residual water or with the organic solvents
